description: Lock GPS to the 1973 NAVSTAR architecture milestone, preserve its constitutive mechanisms, and prevent later GPS fleet logistics from becoming a backwards prerequisite of earlier constellations, route optimization, or drone autonomy.

checks.reason: The scoped milestone is approval and initiation of the integrated NAVSTAR GPS architecture in 1973, not the earlier Transit precursor or later operational service.
NAVSTAR requires an orbiting satellite space segment to broadcast ranging and navigation data.
NAVSTAR requires coordinated multi-satellite geometry and coverage rather than a single orbiting platform.
GPS range measurement requires synchronized high-precision satellite timing.
The satellite-to-receiver radio link carries the ranging and navigation signals.
Accurate GPS timing requires correction for special- and general-relativistic satellite-clock effects.
Integrated circuits enabled small, low-power, low-cost receivers but are not the constitutive satellite-ranging principle.
The node is scoped to documented commercial GPS fleet navigation, tracking, routing, and dispatch adoption rather than earlier non-GPS dispatch.
GPS positioning is constitutive of the explicitly GPS-guided fleet capability.
Motor vehicles and their routes, dispatch, and deliveries are constitutive of the scoped fleet capability.
The documented GPS fleet navigation and tracking capability does not require a broader enterprise supply-chain suite.
Multi-satellite navigation systems predated the 1973 NAVSTAR program, so GPS cannot be a prerequisite for the general constellation class.
The scoped milestone is the first coordinated Vela pair launched on October 17, 1963.
A satellite constellation necessarily consists of multiple artificial satellites operated together.
The 1963 Vela constellation predates the 1999 CubeSat standard by decades.
Vela demonstrates coordinated constellation coverage using detection satellites outside the communications application class.
Fleet-route optimization was formulated in 1959 and does not require a later GPS fleet application.
Operations research remains the direct methodological lineage for the truck-routing optimization milestone.
Autonomous aircraft navigation and control do not require the later commercial GPS fleet-logistics workflow.
Mobile-robot navigation remains the relevant localization, planning, and control lineage.
